topic.what: American College of Gastroenterology clinical guideline for the treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection, addressing declining efficacy of clarithromycin-based triple therapy and recommending bismuth quadruple therapy as a first-line alternative in areas of high clarithromycin resistance.
topic.how: Systematic review and expert consensus using GRADE methodology, covering diagnostic testing, first-line and salvage treatment regimens, retreatment strategies, antibiotic resistance considerations, and special populations.
topic.why_matters: The definitive US guideline that acknowledged the failure of traditional clarithromycin triple therapy due to rising resistance and repositioned bismuth quadruple therapy and concomitant therapy as preferred first-line options.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Local resistance rate above which clarithromycin triple therapy should not be used empirically
characteristics.value: Clarithromycin triple therapy should be abandoned when local resistance exceeds 15%
characteristics.vs_baseline: Previous guidelines did not specify a clear resistance threshold
characteristics.evidence: Meta-analyses of eradication rate decline with resistance
characteristics.context: US clarithromycin resistance estimated at >15% in many regions

characteristics.what_it_measures: Eradication rate of bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline)
characteristics.value: Approximately 85-90% eradication rate, largely unaffected by clarithromycin resistance
characteristics.vs_baseline: Comparable or superior to clarithromycin triple in high-resistance settings

characteristics.what_it_measures: Age below which non-invasive test-and-treat is recommended for uninvestigated dyspepsia
characteristics.value: Test-and-treat with non-invasive testing for patients under 60 without alarm features
characteristics.vs_baseline: Previous ACG guideline used age 55 as cutoff
characteristics.evidence: Cost-effectiveness analyses and cancer risk data
characteristics.context: US population; alarm features warrant endoscopy regardless of age

good_for: Selecting first-line therapy based on resistance patterns
not_for: Regions with very different resistance epidemiology (e.g., sub-Saharan Africa)
requires: Knowledge of local clarithromycin resistance rates (or assumption >15%)
Access to bismuth subsalicylate/subcitrate and tetracycline
Follow-up testing to confirm eradication (UBT or stool antigen)

novelty: First major US guideline to formally discourage empirical clarithromycin triple therapy in high-resistance areas
Elevated bismuth quadruple therapy to co-first-line status
Introduced concomitant quadruple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin + metronidazole) as alternative first-line
Emphasized 14-day treatment durations over shorter courses for all regimens
Provided salvage therapy algorithms for treatment-refractory cases
open_problems: Local resistance surveillance infrastructure lacking in most US regions
Tetracycline and bismuth availability issues in some markets
Optimal approach when multiple prior treatment failures occur
Vonoprazan not addressed (not yet available in US at time of publication)
Levofloxacin salvage therapy complicated by rising fluoroquinolone resistance
